data_caveat: Most JSON items are single-blob ASR transcripts (conversations 94/155, all announcements, all lectures) with no speaker labels and no questions. A minority are OCR'd module items with multi-turn structure + 2 questions. ASR also concatenates adjacent audio clips into one item (lectures >330w, conversations >150w, announcements >150w are merge artifacts) — these are excluded from length targets via clean filters.
postfix_2026_07_10: Current values from paradigm-2026-07-10-postfix.json (listening_lc.gen n=64, listening_la.gen n=72, listening_lat.gen n=137). Live node counts: LC 69 / LA 75 / LAT 141 / LCR 318. CLOSED (bank regenerated): LC median 93 words / 6 turns / named_speaker_count 0; LAT median 256 words (was 167 — the old 'LARGEST GAP'), you_address_opener 46.72% (was 86%), rhetorical_q_opener 14.6% (was 43%). PARTIAL (prompt/mechanism fixed, bank pending regen — NOT a regression): LA salutation_opener_rate 88.89% (real 20.51%; stock_* all 0, OPENER_DECK not yet regenerated into bank), LC greeting_opener 31.25% / peer_topic_opener 6.25% (first-turn peer-opener guidance added, bank not shifted). DEAD-CODE maps_to fixes: (1) LA openers — OPENING_PATTERNS deleted (was never consumed), live lever is OPENER_DECK (laPromptBuilder.js:127) hard-assigned :423-424 + prompt :498; (2) LC length/turns — DIFFICULTY_TIERS:161-179 is dead for length (only used :345-363 for difficulty->Q type), live lever is prompt body :496-503.

dimensions.name: conversation_length_words
dimensions.target: median 89, mean 90, typical band 68-102 (p10-p90)
dimensions.detector: sum words across conversation[].text; OCR-module and audio-ASR subsets agree (89 vs 89 median). Exclude >150w as ASR merge artifacts.
examples: 2026-01-21_m1_conversation1 (71w): Man: Hi. I need to return this sweater. I bought it yesterday, but it's too small for me. Woman: Sure. Do you have the receipt? Man: Yes, I have it right here. Woman: Great. We can process the return. Do you want to exchange it for a larger size or get a refund? Man: I'd like to exchange it, please. Woman: No problem. I will get another in that color for you from our stock. Man: Thank you.

2026-02-10_m1_conversation2 (96w, peer): A: Did you end up seeing that movie yesterday with your friend? B: Yes... There were lots of unexpected twists, but it was too long. A: Should I see it? B: I'm not sure I'd pay money to see it in the theater. Just wait a few months and it will be out on streaming.
BOUNDARY/COUNTER (ASR merge, EXCLUDE): 2026-03-20_audio_conversation8 (323w) = a chess-club conversation concatenated with an unrelated archaeology podcast — not a real single item.
dimensions.gap: CLOSED — postfix median 93 (real 89, was 138). Bank regenerated to the real 68-102 band.
dimensions.maps_to: prompt body lcPromptBuilder.js:496-503 (RECALIBRATED 'median 89 words / 6 turns … 68-102 words total' — the live length lever). NOT DIFFICULTY_TIERS:161-179 — that is DEAD CODE (its 80-200w tier strings are only used :345-363 for difficulty->Q1/Q2 type assignment, never injected as a length spec; confirmed 2026-07-10).

dimensions.name: conversation_turn_count
dimensions.unit: content turns (speaker exchanges, excluding 'Listen to a conversation' lead-in)
dimensions.target: median 6, mean 5.6, band 4-7 (p10-p90)
dimensions.detector: OCR exam_txt: count Man:/Woman: speaker tags per Conversation block, minus 1 lead-in tag. Cross-checked vs 5 JSON multi-turn items (turns 5,6,7,7,7).
examples: 2026-01-21_m1_conversation1: 6 content exchanges (Man/Woman alternating)
2026-01-21_m2_conversation2: 5 turns (shortest observed in JSON)
Real conversations are short: 2 speakers strictly alternate, no third speaker ever observed.
dimensions.gap: CLOSED — postfix median 6 (real 6, was 9).
dimensions.maps_to: prompt body lcPromptBuilder.js:496-503 ('4-7 turns total (each speaker ~2-4 turns)'). The old :494 '8-12 turns' string was replaced by this RECALIBRATED block; DIFFICULTY_TIERS turn counts are dead code (see conversation_length_words).

dimensions.name: conversation_relationship
dimensions.target: peer/social 78-97% (overwhelming majority); service-transaction 3-12%; advising/office-hours ~5-10%
dimensions.detector: Heuristic on joined turn text: service signals (receipt/refund/return/front desk/booking/appointment/office hours) vs peer (recommendations, personal plans, opinions, shared experience). Two thresholds bracket the true value.
examples: PEER: 2026-01-27_m1_conversation1-B couch recommendations for a dorm room ('I've been thinking about getting a new couch... Do you have any recommendations?')
PEER: 2026-04-13_audio_conversation10 restaurant/vacation chat ('Have you checked out the new restaurant downtown? The sushi is made by an award-winning chef...')
SERVICE (minority): 2026-01-21_m1_conversation1 sweater return at a store counter.

current.source: postfix first-turn proxy (service/peer split not re-measured)

dimensions.gap: PARTIALLY ADDRESSED. A RECALIBRATED 'First turn' block (lcPromptBuilder.js:505-513) now pushes peer openers, but the bank has not fully shifted — greeting_opener 31.25% (real 10.32%) still ~3x real; peer_topic_opener 6.25% (real 11.61%). Prompt-side fixed, bank pending regen — watch next round, not a regression.
dimensions.maps_to: lcPromptBuilder.js:20-71 SCENARIO_POOL + prompt body :505-513 (first-turn peer-opener rule)

dimensions.name: conversation_topic_domain
dimensions.target: everyday/social life topics dominate: shopping & returns, food/dining & cooking tips, music & arts, hobbies/clubs, travel/plans, transport schedules, roommate/dorm life, part-time jobs, errands. Mix of campus-set and general-life; NOT dominated by campus-service problems.
dimensions.detector: manual deep-read of 16 spread items + topic keyword scan
examples: music recommendations: 2026-04-28_audio_conversation9 ('I'm getting bored listening to the same old pop singers. Any recommendations?')
cooking/dinner-party tips: 2026-03-23_audio_conversation9 ('I want to buy some cheese for the dinner party... Parmesan if you're serving pasta')
part-time job anecdote: 2026-05-06_audio_conversation1 (potato chip factory, machine 'went haywire')

current.note: generated topics are campus-service problems (noise complaint, parking permit, broken printer, missing package, WiFi)

dimensions.gap: Real topics are lighter, more personal/social; generated topics center on campus logistics problems with a defined resolution. Register/topic mismatch reinforces relationship gap.
dimensions.maps_to: lcPromptBuilder.js:20-71 SCENARIO_POOL situations

dimensions.name: conversation_register_contractions
dimensions.target: ~4.5 / 100w (99% of items contain at least one contraction)
dimensions.detector: regex over ~40 common English contractions / total words. Verified: physics lecture 5 contractions/249w computed correctly.
examples: Natural spoken markers in real conv: 'I'd like to', 'I'm not sure', 'it's too small', 'they're very flexible', 'You're a lifesaver'.
dimensions.gap: Generated conversations are slightly over-casual (6.2 vs 4.5) — acceptable but trending high; real spoken conv is contraction-rich but not saturated.
dimensions.maps_to: lcPromptBuilder.js:500 ('Use contractions FREELY'); :502 fillers/reactions

dimensions.name: announcement_length_words
dimensions.target: median 83, mean 82, band 71-88 (p10-p90), range 46-150
dimensions.detector: word count of transcript; exclude >150w as ASR merges (audio_announcement4/5 high-index items).
examples: 2026-01-21_m1_announcement3-B (83w, campus radio): 'Due to yesterday's heavy rainstorms, a major leak has developed in the roof of the library... the library will be closed until noon on Wednesday...'
2026-01-21_m1_announcement2 (classroom): 'I'm pleased to announce that we'll have a guest speaker next week as part of our unit on environmental policy. Dr. Maya Tan will join us on Thursday...'
BOUNDARY (ASR merge, EXCLUDE): 2026-03-20_audio_announcement4 (249w) = two announcements glued.
dimensions.gap: Postfix median 98 (real 84, was 92) — now runs slightly LONGER than real. Prompt's '80-120 words' overshoots; tighten toward ~84.
dimensions.maps_to: laPromptBuilder.js:8 + :505 + :533 ('80-120 words, 5-7 sentences')

dimensions.name: announcement_opener_pattern
dimensions.target: Attention... 21%; Good morning/afternoon... 17%; Direct statement ~12%; first-person professor (I'm pleased/I have/I want) ~6%; Thank you... 6%; Due to/Since (cause) 6%; We... (institutional) 5%; Hello/Welcome + rhetorical-question hooks present. NO single opener exceeds ~21%.
dimensions.detector: strip 'Man:/Woman:' label AND embedded setting lead-in ('in a university classroom.'), then classify first 45 chars. (~10% residual double-prefix noise.)
examples: Attention: 'Attention students, faculty, and staff. Due to the upcoming Spring Alumni Weekend...'
Good morning (classroom): 'Good morning, students. Before we get started today...'
Due to (radio): 'Due to yesterday's heavy rainstorms, a major leak has developed...'
dimensions.gap: MECHANISM FIXED, BANK PENDING REGEN. Stock-phrase tropes are gone (all stock_* 0, real still has 1 'pleased to announce'), but salutation_opener_rate is still 88.89% (real 20.51%) because the bank was not yet regenerated with OPENER_DECK. Treat as stale bank, not a live regression.
dimensions.maps_to: DEAD-CODE FIX (2026-07-10): OPENING_PATTERNS (rate:64 Attention) was never consumed and has been DELETED. Live opener lever = laPromptBuilder.js:127 OPENER_DECK = ['direct','direct','direct','direct','cause','professor','attention','greeting'] (2/8 salutation => ~25% expected), hard-assigned in buildLAPrompt :423-424, plus prompt body :498 ('salutations are only ~1 in 4').

dimensions.name: announcement_context_purpose
dimensions.target: classroom/course 36% (professor: guest speaker, syllabus, assignment); campus_event/activity 29%; facility/logistics 12%; tour/exhibit 9% (art exhibit walkthrough); campus/university radio is a recurring SETTING (~9 of 35 specified). NOT dominated by facility-change.
dimensions.detector: classify setting + transcript keywords. Settings are richly specified in real data (75/78 non-generic).
examples: classroom/course: 'As you know, this is the final course required for your business degree. The course will operate as an independent study...'
tour/exhibit: 'Listen to an announcement at a school art exhibit. Thank you for touring this special collection of paintings...'
campus radio: 'Listen to an announcement on a campus radio station. Due to yesterday's heavy rainstorms...'

current.note: CONTEXT_POOL = facility_change 21% / academic_event 21% / campus_activity 14% / logistics/career/guest_speaker/info_session 7% each. No 'classroom/course' type, no 'art exhibit' type, no 'campus radio' setting.

dimensions.gap: Real's #1 context (classroom/course professor announcements, 36%) is ABSENT from the generated pool. Real also has art-exhibit walkthroughs and campus-radio news that the pool lacks. Generated over-indexes facility_change (21%) vs real 12%.
dimensions.maps_to: laPromptBuilder.js:21-109 CONTEXT_POOL

dimensions.name: announcement_register_contractions
dimensions.target: ~1.9 / 100w (71% of items contain at least one) — semi-formal, lightly contracted
dimensions.detector: contraction regex / words
examples: 'we'll have a guest speaker', 'we're proud of it', 'don't miss' — contractions present but sparse vs conversation.

dimensions.name: lecture_length_words
dimensions.target: median 250, mean 246, band 220-258 (p10-p90), range 192-279. Very tight single-peak.
dimensions.detector: word count of transcript; exclude >330w as ASR merges (6 items, all high-index audio_talk6/7/10 — verified to be 2-3 lectures concatenated). Clean ceiling ~280.
examples: 2026-01-27_m1_lecture1-B (245w, art history): 'Expressionism is an influential modernist art movement that emerged in the early 20th century. This movement is characterized by its focus on representing emotional experience rather than physical reality...'
2026-01-21_m1_lecture1 (249w, physics): 'We've been talking about the law of inertia... So how does a cyclist avoid this? The key is to lean in...'
BOUNDARY (ASR merge, EXCLUDE): 2026-04-06_audio_talk7 (505w) = a history-podcast on Louis Latimer + an art-class lecture on Kandinsky + a glacier fragment.
dimensions.gap: CLOSED — the former 'LARGEST GAP' is resolved. Postfix median 256 (real 250, was 167); bank regenerated into the tight real 220-258 band. (A recap_opener subtype was added in Batch 6 but bank still shows recap_opener_rate 0% vs real 8.85% — minor, pending regen.)

dimensions.name: lecture_academic_domain
dimensions.target: arts_humanities (art/art-history/music/architecture/literature/creative-writing) 30%; social_sci (psych/econ/business/marketing/sociology) 20%; phys_earth_sci (physics/geology/environmental/general science) 17%; life_sci (biology/marine/ecology/health) 16%; history_humanities (history/archaeology/anthropology) 10%. Arts+humanities combined ~40%. SINGLE most common specific field = ART HISTORY (12%, 13/113).
dimensions.detector: extract explicit field label from setting + transcript lead-in ('in a/at a/on a X class/podcast'), map to broad domain. Verified: 13 art-history all explicitly labeled.
examples: art history: 'Listen to a talk in an art history class. Last week we talked about realism, in which artists aim...'
music: 'Listen to a lecture at a music class. If you're familiar with ancient Greece, you know how...'
architecture: 'Listen to a talk in an architecture class. Have you ever thought about how ancient concrete structures...'
dimensions.gap: Generated bank is dominated by science + psychology (bio/psych/cog-psych/social-psych/neuro/eco = ~64%); arts+humanities only ~14% with just 1 art_history, 0 music, 0 architecture, 0 literature. Real's largest bucket is arts/humanities (40%), with art history the single most frequent field. Major topic-domain gap.
dimensions.maps_to: latPromptBuilder.js:19+ TOPIC_POOL (biology .12, ecology .12, psychology .15, social_psych .10, cognitive_psych ... — sciences/psych heavily weighted; arts underweighted)

dimensions.name: lecture_register_contractions
dimensions.target: ~1.2 / 100w — academic, lightly conversational (much lower than conversation's 4.5)
dimensions.detector: contraction regex / words; verified on physics lecture (5/249 = 2.0, one of the more conversational).
examples: Real lecture register: 'We've been talking about...', 'we don't just ride in straight lines, do we?', 'this approach isn't perfect' — sparse contractions, formal-but-spoken.
dimensions.gap: Generated lectures are 4x too casual (5.0 vs 1.2/100w) — they read like conversations ('Okay, so today we're diving into... Now, you might think... And here's the thing... Actually...'). Real academic talks are measured and lecture-like.
dimensions.maps_to: latPromptBuilder.js:9 ('Conversational academic register (contractions, you, rhetorical questions)') — over-applied

dimensions.name: lecture_second_person_and_rhetorical_opener
dimensions.unit: % of lectures using 'you/your' in opening 160 chars; % opening with a rhetorical question
dimensions.target: direct 'you' address in opener ~21%; rhetorical-question opener ~14% — used as occasional hooks, not the default
dimensions.detector: scan first 160 chars (after stripping label + setting lead-in) for you/your and for '?'
examples: rhetorical hook: 'When you think of 19th century inventors, what comes to mind?'
most real lectures open declaratively: 'Expressionism is an influential modernist art movement...' / 'Migratory birds travel vast distances...'
dimensions.gap: CLOSED — postfix you_address_opener 46.72% (real 42.48%, was 86%), rhetorical_q_opener 14.6% (real 18.58%, was 43%). Both now match real; no longer too chatty.

dimensions.name: shortResponse_prompt_length
dimensions.target: median 8, mean 7.9, band 6-11 (p10-p90), range 3-14
dimensions.detector: word count of each prompt (after stripping leaked 'Man:/Woman:' labels)
examples: 'Where can I find the class notes?' (6w)
'Can you turn down the volume?' (6w)
'I don't know how many people will join us tonight for study group.' (13w)
dimensions.gap: Generated prompts ~25% longer (median 10 vs 8). Real prompts are crisp one-liners.
dimensions.maps_to: lcrPromptBuilder.js speaker-sentence construction (no explicit length cap toward 8w)

dimensions.name: shortResponse_sentence_type
dimensions.unit: % question vs statement; within questions, wh- vs yes/no
dimensions.target: questions 74% (wh-questions 49%, yes/no-questions 24%); statements 26%; imperatives ~1%
dimensions.detector: ends-with-'?' => question; first word who/what/where/when/why/how/which/whose => wh; else yes/no. Strip leaked speaker labels first (critical — pre-strip undercounted wh). Hand-verified first 12 (6 wh / 3 y-n / 3 stmt).
examples: wh-Q: 'Who is the guest lecturer today?' / 'Why is the air conditioner in the dorm not working?'
yes/no-Q: 'Did you enjoy the gardening class?'
STATEMENT (responds-to): 'Peter said he would look for supplies.' / 'Maybe the power went out for this whole area.'
dimensions.gap: Question/statement split is close (68/32 vs 74/26), BUT the wh- vs yes/no balance is badly off: generated wh-questions = 7% vs real 49%. The generated bank almost never opens with Who/Where/Why/How. Real short-response leans heavily on wh-questions whose distractors answer a DIFFERENT wh-dimension.
dimensions.maps_to: lcrPromptBuilder.js:12 ('statements 30%, questions 70%') — question/statement ratio is right; wh/yn balance is unspecified and drifted to yes/no.

dimensions.name: shortResponse_campus_vs_general
dimensions.target: ~40% campus/academic, ~60% general everyday — more general-life than the generated LCR bank
dimensions.detector: campus keyword scan (class/notes/professor/dorm/tutor/study group/exam/library/...)
examples: campus: 'Who is the tutor in the student success center today?'
general: 'Can you turn down the volume?' / 'Maybe the power went out for this whole area.'
dimensions.gap: Generated LCR is 65% campus vs real short-response ~40%. Real prompts include more domestic/general-life situations (noise, power, volume, errands).
dimensions.maps_to: lcrPromptBuilder.js:17-70 SCENARIO_POOL (campus_academic .375 + campus_daily .3125 = ~69% campus)

dimensions.name: answer_position_distribution
dimensions.target: A 24% / B 28% / C 28% / D 20% — mild bias toward middle (B/C), away from D; NOT perfectly uniform
dimensions.detector: parse single-letter answers from listening sections of all answer-key files (.codex-tmp/exam_txt/*参考答案*).
examples: Across 658 real listening answers, B and C are each ~28%, D is the rarest at ~20%.
dimensions.gap: Generated banks over-cluster on B (LAT B=44%, LC B=37% vs real B=28%) and starve D (LC D=11% vs real 20%). The prompt's strict position pre-assignment is not fully obeyed by the model. Real ETS itself is mildly B/C-biased, so target B/C ~28% / A ~24% / D ~20% rather than forcing 25/25/25/25.
dimensions.maps_to: all builders: 'Answer position pre-assignment' (lc:331-338, la:364-371, lat header, lcr header)

dimensions.name: question_count_per_item
dimensions.target: conversation 2; announcement 2; lecture 4; short-response is a standalone 1-prompt+4-option item
dimensions.detector: OCR exam_txt: count numbered '?' stems per section block. (mode is the truth; lower counts are OCR truncation, '7' values are ASR merges.)
examples: Conversation1 -> Q13 'Why is the man returning the sweater?', Q14 'What will the woman most likely do next?'
Lecture1 (physics) -> 4 questions (purpose, suggest-about-turning, leaning-in-helps, professionals-try-to-do).
dimensions.gap: Generated matches real (2/2/4). No gap.
dimensions.maps_to: lc:537-566 (2 Qs); la:568-594 (2 Qs); lat:11 ('4 questions'); lcr (single response item)

dimensions.name: question_stem_phrasing
dimensions.subtype: all (DEFERRED — stems live in image PDFs)
dimensions.target: Conversation: 'Why is the man/woman ...?', 'What will the man/woman most likely do next?', 'What are the man and the woman talking about?', 'What is the man's problem?', 'What does the man imply when he says, "..."?' (replay/quote), 'What is the man's attitude toward ...?'. Lecture: 'What is the purpose/main topic of the talk?', 'What does the speaker mainly discuss?', 'What does the speaker suggest/imply about X?', 'Why does the speaker mention X?'. Announcement: 'What is the purpose of the announcement?', 'What will the speaker probably do next?', 'What can be inferred about X?', 'What ... does the speaker emphasize/stress?'.

distribution.note: type-distribution unreliable from OCR (short-response prompts leak into blocks; classifier noisy)

dimensions.detector: OCR exam_txt stem recovery; stem TYPE classification is noisy and left partial.
examples: 'What does the man imply when he says, "I'll bring a blanket too"?' (replay/function Q — a type the LC prompt does not generate)
'At the end of the conversation, what is the man's attitude toward the woman's idea?' (attitude Q)
'Why does the speaker mention butter?' (function Q — hallmark of real lecture questions)

current.note: LC stems lack the quote-replay ('what does X imply when he says') and attitude types; LAT/LA stems otherwise overlap real phrasings well.

dimensions.gap: Add quote-replay ('What does the man/woman imply when he says, "..."?') and attitude stems to LC; emphasize 'Why does the speaker mention X?' function stems in LAT. Full audit deferred until question PDFs are OCR'd cleanly.
dimensions.maps_to: lc:182-204 STEM_PATTERNS; la:249-272 STEM_PATTERNS; lat STEM patterns

dimensions.name: speaker_label_convention
dimensions.target: Real transcripts use generic 'Man' / 'Woman' (exactly 2, strictly alternating). No first names, no roles in the transcript.
dimensions.detector: distinct speaker values in JSON multi-turn items + OCR (Man:/Woman: universally).
examples: Man: / Woman: in every OCR conversation block and all 5 JSON multi-turn items.
dimensions.gap: CLOSED — postfix named_speaker_count 0; bank now uses generic Man/Woman (was first names).
dimensions.maps_to: lcPromptBuilder.js:430-431 (transcript labelled 'Woman:'/'Man:') + prompt body :499 ('no first names; question stems refer to the man/the woman')

correlations.name: register tracks subtype, and the generated bank collapses the register gradient
correlations.detail: Real contraction density is a clean ladder: conversation 4.5 > announcement 1.9 > lecture 1.2 per 100w. The generated bank inverts/flattens it: LAT 5.0 (too high) and LA 0.40 (too low). 'Authenticity of register' = matching this gradient. A lecture that sounds like a chat, or an announcement that sounds bureaucratic, is the #1 register tell.

correlations.name: length, turns, and casualness co-define a real conversation
correlations.detail: Real conversations are jointly short (89w), few-turn (6), peer/social (78-97%), and contraction-rich (4.5/100w). The generated bank is long (138w), many-turn (9), service-transactional (52%) — every conversation dimension drifts the same direction: toward a longer, more formal service-desk dialogue. Fixing topic/relationship (peer chat) will naturally shorten length and turns.

correlations.name: arts/humanities dominance is the lecture-domain authenticity signal
correlations.detail: Real lectures are 40% arts+humanities (art history alone 12%, the single most common field), only ~33% sciences. The generated bank is ~64% sciences/psychology. A bank that 'feels like real TOEFL listening' must over-weight art history, music, architecture, history — not biology/psychology.

correlations.name: what makes short-response hard = wh-question with cross-dimension distractors
correlations.detail: Real short-response is 49% wh-questions; the trap is distractors that answer a DIFFERENT wh-word (Where -> answered with When/How-long/Format). The generated bank is 61% yes/no with only 7% wh, losing the dominant real difficulty mechanism.

deferred.item: question stem TYPE distribution (main_idea / detail / inference / function / attitude / replay-quote ratios per subtype)
deferred.why: Stems live in the 听力 image PDFs; structured JSON carries only ~15% of items' questions. OCR recovery of stems works (32 conv / 18 ann / 86 lec verbatim stems recovered) but type-classification is noisy (short-response prompts leak into section blocks; many stem variants). Verbatim phrasings ARE captured; reliable type ratios need a dedicated clean OCR pass on the question PDFs.

deferred.item: MCQ answer-option text & distractor trap-logic for LC/LA/LAT (the 4 options per question)
deferred.why: Options are rendered in image PDFs, not in the audio-derived JSON. .codex-tmp/ocr/ DOES contain option text for short-response and could be mined, but parsing is messy (no A/B/C/D labels, OCR artifacts like 'I'mnotsure'). Distractor-type distribution therefore cannot be measured reliably from current structured data — only the short-response trap pattern (cross-wh-dimension) is established from one fully-read exemplar.

Real items carry no difficulty label. The prompts assume 30/45/25; real data offers no ground-truth split to validate this. Length is the only proxy and it is unimodal (no clear easy/medium/hard clusters), so the tier split is unverifiable — do NOT treat 30/45/25 as evidence-based.

deferred.item: conversation relationship exact ratio (peer vs service)
deferred.why: Bracketed at 78-97% peer by two heuristics but no labeled ground truth; the true value is clearly 'large majority peer' but the precise number is partial.
